rationale: Dhar and Wertenbroch (2000) is discussed multiple times beyond a list citation, with its core finding and mechanism paraphrased in detail. The citing article explicitly relies on it to support a central boundary-condition claim: affect-rich hedonic goods produce stronger reluctance to give up than utilitarian goods. It is also used to help interpret Novemsky and Kahneman's no-loss-aversion-for-money result, by contrasting money with hedonic goods. This is a meaningful conceptual support role, not a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Dhar and Wertenbroch (2000) as empirical evidence that forfeiture versus acquisition framing has stronger loss-aversion effects for hedonic than utilitarian options. It leverages that evidence to argue that affective attachment moderates endowment effects and to explain weaker loss aversion for money.
